en.extract:
The grosbeak starling (Scissirostrum dubium), also known as the grosbeak myna, finch-billed myna, or scissor-billed starling, is a species of starling in the family Sturnidae. It is monotypic in the genus Scissirostrum. It is endemic to Sulawesi, Indonesia, where its natural habitat is tropical lowland, and sometimes subtropical montane, lightly wooded forest areas and wetlands. It is threatened in the wild by habitat loss, and by birds being captured for the cagebird trade.
This species nests in colonies, which frequently contain hundreds of pairs. Its nests are bored in rotting or dying tree trunks in woodpecker style. It eats fruit, insects, and grain. Grosbeak starlings are highly vocal, at their colonies and in feeding flocks.
The grosbeak starling was first described by the English ornithologist John Latham in 1801 under the binomial name Lanius dubium.
New populations, derived from escaped cagebirds, have been found breeding in Kalimantan in Borneo, and in Java.


References

The '''grosbeak starling''' ('''''Scissirostrum dubium'''''), also known as the '''grosbeak myna''', '''finch-billed myna''', or '''scissor-billed starling''', is a species of [[starling]] in the family [[Sturnidae]]. It is [[monotypic]] in the genus '''''Scissirostrum'''''.<ref name=Feare>Feare, C., Craig, A., Croucher, B., Shields, C., & Komolphalin, K. 1998. ''Starlings and Mynas''. Christopher Helm. {{ISBN|0-7136-3961-X}}</ref> It is [[endemic]] to [[Sulawesi]], [[Indonesia]],<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" /> where its natural [[habitat]] is [[tropical]] [[lowland]], and sometimes [[subtropical]] [[montane]], lightly wooded [[forest]] areas and [[wetland]]s.<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" /> It is threatened in the wild by habitat loss, and by birds being captured for the [[Wildlife trade|cagebird trade]].<ref name="Iqbal"/>

This species nests in colonies, which frequently contain hundreds of pairs. Its nests are bored in rotting or dying tree trunks in woodpecker style. It eats fruit, insects, and grain.<ref name=Feare/> Grosbeak starlings are highly vocal, at their colonies and in feeding flocks.<ref name=Feare/>

The grosbeak starling was first described by the English ornithologist [[John Latham (ornithologist)|John Latham]] in 1801 under the [[binomial name]] ''Lanius dubium''.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Latham | first=John | year=1801 | title=Supplementum indicis ornithologici sive systematis ornithologiae | place=London | publisher=Leigh & Sotheby | language=Latin | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/33261392 | page=xviii }}</ref>

New populations, derived from escaped cagebirds, have been found breeding in [[Kalimantan]] in [[Borneo]], and in [[Java]].<ref name="Iqbal">{{cite journal | last1=Iqbal | first1=Muhammad | last2=Prawira | first2=Aril Magda | last3=Wicaksono | first3=Gusti | last4=Taufiqurrahman | first4=Imam | title=Records of Grosbeak Starling ''Scissirostrum dubium'' from Kalimantan, Borneo, and its widespread occurrence on Java. BirdingASIA 29: 109–113. | journal=BirdingAsia | volume=29 | date=2018-09-01 | issn=1744-537X | pages=109–113 | url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/352245784_Records_of_Grosbeak_Starling_Scissirostrum_dubium_from_Kalimantan_Borneo_and_its_widespread_occurrence_on_Java_BirdingASIA_29_109-113 | access-date=2025-08-24}}</ref>
